sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=4, col=43), SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 16, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 15 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After selecting purple and pressing SPACE:
- Node 12 (gray-bordered rect) changed to purple
- Node 13 is purple with 50 pixels
- Node 14 is white with 50 pixels
So SPACE split the 100-pixel white rectangle into two 50-pixel rectangles: purple on top, white on bottom. The top half takes the color of the gray-bordered rect (which was just set to purple).
So the pattern is:
1. Select a color using the palette (changes the gray-bordered rect)
2. SPACE splits the rectangle below into two halves, with the top half taking the selected color
The reference panel shows: white on top, purple on bottom.
Currently I have: purple on top, white on bottom (the opposite!)
I need to get white on top, purple on bottom. So I need to:
1. Select white
2. SPACE to split → white on top, (bottom half keeps its color which was white... hmm)
Wait, let me reconsider. The current state is:
- Gray-bordered purple rect
- Purple 5×10 rect (node 13)
- White 5×10 rect (node 14)
The reference shows white above purple. If I select white and SPACE, the purple rect (node 13) would be split into white (top) + purple (bottom)? No, that doesn't make sense either.
Actually, looking at the pattern more carefully:
- First SPACE on a 10×10 white rect → split into purple (top) + white (bottom). The gray-bordered rect was purple at that time.
- Second SPACE on a 10×10 white rect → split into white (top) + white (bottom). The gray-bordered rect was white at that time.
- Third SPACE on a 10×10 white rect → split into purple (top) + white (bottom). The gray-bordered rect was purple.
So SPACE splits the rectangle below the gray-bordered one, and the top half takes the color of the gray-bordered rect.
Currently I have two 5×10 rectangles. The reference has white on top, purple on bottom. Currently it's purple on top, white on bottom.
Maybe I need to merge them back and then split with the right color. But I don't know how to merge them.
Wait - maybe I need to think about this differently. The reference panel shows white above purple. Maybe the goal is to have the gray-bordered rectangle be white and the rectangle below be purple. Currently the gray-bordered is purple and below is purple+white.
Let me try: select white (so gray-bordered becomes white), then SPACE. This should split the purple rect below into white (top) + purple (bottom). That would give white on top, purple on bottom - matching the reference!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: gray-bordered purple, then purple 5x10, then white 5x10
# I want: white on top, purple on bottom (matching reference)
# Select white and SPACE to split the purple rect below into white(top) + purple(bottom)

result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 37}])
print(f"Select white: {last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

result = action(['SPACE'])
print(f"After SPACE:")
print(f"  done={last_action_result.get('done')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['id'] in [8, 12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  id=13 color=W pixels=100 boundary=[[34, 27], [34, 36], [43, 36], [43, 27]]
  id=14 color=c pixels=53 boundary=[[63, 0], [63, 52]]
